speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're doing a deep dive into GitHub Projects, especially the modern Projects experience sometimes called Projects v2. Think of this as GitHub's built-in project management system for issues, pull requests, and draft work items, all in one place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is for people who already know GitHub issues and maybe milestones, but want the bigger planning picture?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And it's especially helpful if you want one living workspace that can pull work from one repository or many repositories, then show it as a table, a board, or a roadmap. We'll also keep accessibility front and center: keyboard use, screen reader behavior, and low-vision navigation.

speaker: ALEX
text: One quick framing note. This appendix is a companion to the earlier material on labels, milestones, and projects. Here we're going beyond the basics.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And before we get into the how, what changed with the newer Projects system?

speaker: ALEX
text: A lot. GitHub had an older generation sometimes called classic projects. That older system was mostly Kanban only. The newer Projects version gives you three layouts: Board, Table, and Roadmap.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not just sticky notes in columns anymore.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. You also get custom fields now, which is a huge shift. Instead of just title and labels and assignees, you can add your own fields like text notes, numeric estimates, dates, single-select choices like priority, and iteration fields for sprint-style planning.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about automation?

speaker: ALEX
text: Also improved. Classic projects had very limited built-in behavior. Projects v2 supports built-in workflows and integrates with GitHub Actions. Filtering is much stronger too. You can query by labels, assignees, statuses, and custom field values. Cross-repository tracking is supported, and the API story is more powerful through GraphQL.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone sees old references to Projects Beta?

speaker: ALEX
text: That was the earlier rollout period for this newer system. Today, Projects v2 is the standard one people mean.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, I want to create one. Where can a project live?

speaker: ALEX
text: Two common places. At the organization level, which makes sense when a team wants shared planning across multiple repositories. Or at the repository level, which is narrower and centered on one repo.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's start with organization-level creation.

speaker: ALEX
text: You navigate to the organization page, open the Projects tab, activate the New project button, choose a template or start blank, give the project a name, and activate Create project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What kinds of templates are we talking about?

speaker: ALEX
text: Things like a blank table, blank board, or a more opinionated setup like a feature release template. Templates can save time if you already know the workflow pattern you want.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And from a repository?

speaker: ALEX
text: Very similar. Go to the repository, open its Projects tab, then use Link a project or New project, depending on what GitHub shows there. If you create a new one, the template and naming flow is basically the same.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we pause on the screen reader flow? Because this is one of those spots where sighted walkthroughs can hide important interaction details.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. A typical screen reader path is: go to the org or repo Projects tab, tab to the green New project button, press Enter. The template dialog opens. Use arrow keys to move among template cards, each announcing its name and description. Press Enter on the one you want. Then type the project name in the name field, tab to Create project, press Enter, and you'll land in the new empty project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And after creation, what should users expect?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually an empty project in Table view. There's often a plus Add item control near the bottom. For screen reader users, tabbing to that control is a reliable way to start adding work. For low-vision users, one practical note: at high zoom, that add-item row may be off-screen, so scrolling down matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And visually, the New project button tends to be easy to spot?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, typically a green high-contrast button on the Projects tab.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's talk about the three layouts, because this seems like the heart of the system.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. The default is usually Table view. Think spreadsheet. Each row is an issue, a pull request, or a draft item. Each column is a field: title, status, assignee, labels, priority, dates, repository, or any custom fields you create.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is Table view best at?

speaker: ALEX
text: Bulk editing, sorting, filtering, and scanning lots of structured information quickly. If you want the most information density, this is usually the winner.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And accessibility-wise?

speaker: ALEX
text: It's generally the most accessible layout. Keyboard movement is strong. Common shortcuts and navigation patterns include jumping to the table, moving up and down between rows, left and right between columns, pressing Enter to open the item detail panel in the right sidebar, Escape to close that panel, Space on a cell to edit the value, and F2 on the title cell for inline title editing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does a row sound like in a screen reader?

speaker: ALEX
text: Something like: title first, then field-value pairs. For example, "Fix keyboard trap in modal dialog, Status: In Progress, Assignee: alice, Priority: High, Labels: bug, accessibility." That row-style verbal summary is one reason Table view works so well nonvisually.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone is blind and choosing a default working view, Table is probably the safest choice.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Table first, especially for editing.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about Board view?

speaker: ALEX
text: Board view is the Kanban-style layout. Columns represent status values, and cards sit inside those columns. It's excellent for workflow tracking, work-in-progress conversations, and quick standup-style reviews.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But I know there's an accessibility catch here.

speaker: ALEX
text: There is. Drag-and-drop is not keyboard accessible in the way many users need. So the accessible method for changing a card's column is not dragging. Instead, open the card, find the Status field in the right-side detail panel, activate the status dropdown, choose the new value, and then close the panel.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's such an important pattern: don't imitate the visual gesture if the underlying field can be edited directly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The column is just a visual expression of the Status field. Change the field, and the card moves.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Keyboard-wise, how do people move around the board?

speaker: ALEX
text: Tab commonly moves among columns and cards. Enter on a card opens the detail panel. In some contexts, heading or region navigation can help jump among board areas. And within a card context, Space may toggle selection for bulk actions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: For low-vision users, any practical warning?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Board columns can overflow horizontally, so at high zoom you may need to scroll sideways. Card details like label badges or avatars can become small and crowded, so zooming in on a single card or switching to Table view can be easier.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Roadmap view?

speaker: ALEX
text: Roadmap is a horizontal timeline. Items appear as bars spanning dates, often start dates and due dates, or iteration windows. It's useful for release planning, sprint capacity planning, and communicating timing to stakeholders.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This sounds visually rich, but maybe less accessible directly.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's the right instinct. Roadmap is chart-like, so for screen reader users it's best treated as a visual summary rather than the primary editing surface. The underlying data is still accessible in Table view. So the recommended workflow is: edit and verify in Table, then switch to Roadmap if you want the big timeline picture.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do items get onto the roadmap timeline?

speaker: ALEX
text: They need date information. You can add Date custom fields like Start Date and Due Date, or sometimes use milestone-related timing depending on your setup. Items without dates usually sit outside the timeline grouping.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And low-vision users may need more zoom because those bars can be narrow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's move into custom fields, because this is where Projects starts feeling like a real planning database.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a great way to say it. Projects includes default fields like Title, Assignee, Labels, Milestone, and Repository, but its real power comes from adding your own structured fields.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Walk us through the major field types.

speaker: ALEX
text: Text fields are free-form notes, useful for links, criteria, or reminders. Number fields are for numeric values like estimates or story points. Date fields store deadlines or start dates. Single select gives you a dropdown of predefined choices, great for priority, size, risk, or type. And Iteration fields support sprint or cycle planning.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you create one?

speaker: ALEX
text: From Table view, you usually move to the far end of the header row and activate the plus button that adds a field. Then you choose the field type, provide the field name, and configure options if needed. For single-select, for example, you define the allowed values such as P0, P1, and P2.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about the screen reader path for that?

speaker: ALEX
text: The important pieces are: get into Table view, move across the header row until you reach the add-field control, activate it, select the field type from the menu or dialog, then fill in the naming and option fields. Because this interaction can involve horizontal movement across many columns, it may take some patience, especially in a busy project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And once the field exists, how do you edit a value on an item?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually two ways. In Table view, navigate to the cell and activate it, often with Space, then choose or type the value. Or open the item detail panel and edit the field there in the sidebar. The panel method is often clearer for screen reader users because it exposes labeled controls in a more linear form.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if a learner finds the grid editing model awkward, the sidebar is a good fallback.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much so.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's add work items. What exactly can live inside a project?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three main categories: existing issues, existing pull requests, and draft issues. Drafts are especially handy when you want to capture work before a formal issue exists in a repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you add an existing issue or pull request?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the add-item control in the project, then search for the issue or PR you want. Depending on the project's scope and permissions, you can pull in items from linked repositories or other repos in a cross-repository setup.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And a draft issue?

speaker: ALEX
text: A draft issue is created directly in the project without immediately creating a repository issue. It's useful for planning, brainstorming, backlog triage, or work that isn't ready to be assigned to a repo yet.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then later you can promote it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Promoting a draft turns it into a real GitHub issue in a repository. That's a nice bridge between planning and execution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So a team can start fuzzy, then formalize later.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about bulk editing?

speaker: ALEX
text: That's one of Table view's strengths. You can select multiple items and update shared properties together, such as status, iteration, assignee, or other field values. In practice, this is useful when moving a batch of tasks into a sprint, closing out a release phase, or assigning a common label or priority pattern.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Accessibility tip there?

speaker: ALEX
text: Know how selection is announced, and confirm the set of selected items before applying the bulk change. In any bulk operation, the risk isn't understanding one item; it's accidentally changing many.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's get to automations. What can Projects do on its own?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub Projects includes built-in workflows and can also work with GitHub Actions. The idea is to reduce repetitive project maintenance.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do people access automations?

speaker: ALEX
text: Typically through project settings or a workflows area associated with the project. The exact UI can shift over time, but conceptually you're looking for automation or workflow configuration within the project's controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what kinds of built-in workflows are we talking about?

speaker: ALEX
text: Common examples include automatically adding items to a project when they meet certain conditions, or updating fields based on status changes and project events. The source emphasizes native workflows and GitHub Actions integration rather than treating the project as a totally manual tool.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I noticed auto-add was specifically called out.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Auto-add is especially useful when you want matching issues or pull requests to appear in a project automatically. For example, perhaps all issues with a certain label, or work from a specific repository, should flow into a tracking project without someone manually adding them every time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds powerful, but also like something you'd want to test carefully.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. Start with a narrow rule, verify the results, then expand. Automation should reduce work, not create surprise clutter.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And GitHub Actions integration means you can go beyond built-in toggles?

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Actions can update project fields, move work through states, or synchronize project data with repository events in more advanced workflows. If your team already uses Actions, Projects becomes another place where automation can keep planning data current.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now the sprint story: iterations.

speaker: ALEX
text: Iterations are GitHub's sprint-like field type. Instead of storing just a random date, an iteration field defines repeating cycles, such as two-week sprints.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you create one?

speaker: ALEX
text: Create a custom field and choose Iteration as the type. Then configure the cadence, such as duration and start date. GitHub generates the repeating iteration values from that setup.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And using iterations means assigning each item to one of those sprint buckets?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Once the field exists, items can be assigned to a specific iteration, making it easy to filter by current sprint, next sprint, or backlog. This is often clearer than manually typing sprint names in a text field because the values are structured and date-aware.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are iteration insights?

speaker: ALEX
text: They're the planning benefits you get from that structure: seeing which items are in the current cycle, comparing upcoming cycles, and using roadmap or filtered views to understand sprint load and timing. In other words, the insight comes from consistent structured data.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's not just labeling work "Sprint 4." It's giving GitHub enough semantics to reason about time.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly put.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Views and filters feel like another big productivity layer.

speaker: ALEX
text: They are. A project can have multiple saved views, each showing the same underlying items differently.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So one project, many perspectives.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You might have a Table view for backlog grooming, a Board view for daily status, and a Roadmap view for release timing. You can also create specialized views like "My open work," "Current sprint," or "Only accessibility bugs."

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you create a view?

speaker: ALEX
text: You usually start from the current layout, apply filters, grouping, sorting, and visible fields the way you want, then save that configuration as a named view.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's unpack filter syntax a bit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Projects supports query-style filtering using fields and common GitHub metadata. The exact syntax can include things like status values, labels, assignees, repository names, and custom fields. The important conceptual point is that filtering is expressive. You are not limited to one simple dropdown; you can build meaningful queries.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So for example, I might isolate items assigned to me, with a certain label, in a particular status.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Or only items from one repository inside a cross-repo project. Or only the current iteration. Filters become much more valuable once your project has structured fields.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And grouping?

speaker: ALEX
text: Grouping changes how items are clustered visually. In Table view, you might group by Status, Assignee, Priority, or Iteration. That lets you treat one project almost like several logical sections without creating separate projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Sorting is more straightforward, I assume.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Sorting orders items by one or more fields. Useful examples include due date ascending, priority descending, or recently updated work first. Combined with grouping, sorting can make a large project much easier to read.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Cross-repository projects feel especially important for open source maintainers and org-level planning.

speaker: ALEX
text: Definitely. One of the biggest advantages of Projects v2 is that it can track work from multiple repositories together.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you add a repository to a project?

speaker: ALEX
text: At a high level, you configure the project so it can include items from that repository, then add or auto-add issues and PRs from it. The exact UI wording can vary, but the concept is: this project is not limited to one repo. It can aggregate work across several.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then the Repository field becomes important.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very. Once you're mixing repos, the Repository field tells you where each item came from. It's useful in Table view for filtering, sorting, and basic orientation. If two repositories both have an issue named something vague like "Update docs," the Repository field keeps those items distinguishable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So cross-repo planning without the Repository field visible can get confusing fast.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. In a multi-repo view, showing repository is often a best practice.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the dedicated screen reader navigation reference, because a lot of learners need a reliable mental map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Great idea. Start from the project home page, meaning the list of projects for an organization or repository. Expect a list or grid of project entries. Use standard navigation keys to move through links and buttons, and activate the project you want.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Once you're inside a project, what's the general model?

speaker: ALEX
text: There are a few recurring regions: view selectors or tabs near the top, the main content area showing Table, Board, or Roadmap, filtering controls, and often a detail panel that opens on the right when you activate an item.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So learners should think in regions, not just individual controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Region awareness matters. In general, it helps to locate the current view, then the filter bar, then the content area. When you open an item, understand that focus may move into a right sidebar panel with editable fields.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give us the Table view mental map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Header row of fields across the top. Rows of items below. Arrow navigation can move within the grid. Enter opens details. Space may edit a cell. Because it's grid-like, listen carefully for row and column context so you always know which field you're on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Board view?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think columns, then cards within columns. Tab often moves through cards and related controls. Enter opens the selected card in the detail panel. Remember: if you need to move a card between columns accessibly, edit its Status field in the panel instead of trying to drag.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Tell us about the detail panel.

speaker: ALEX
text: This right sidebar appears from any view after opening an item. It's often the most linear and therefore accessible place to review and edit title, status, assignees, labels, custom fields, and other metadata. Escape commonly closes it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the filter bar?

speaker: ALEX
text: The filter bar is where you type or refine project queries. For screen reader users, that's a powerful control because it can quickly reduce noise. If a project feels overwhelming, filtering down to one assignee, one status, one repo, or one iteration can make the experience much more manageable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I'm intrigued by the accessibility agent command mentioned here: slash project-status. What is that for?

speaker: ALEX
text: The idea is an accessibility-oriented helper that summarizes the state of a project in a compact, textual way. Instead of navigating every visual element to answer basic status questions, you ask for a project-status style summary.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What kind of information would it show?

speaker: ALEX
text: Typically things like counts by status, notable items, upcoming dates or sprint context, and a plain-language snapshot of project health or progress. It's meant to surface the information someone might otherwise gather by visually scanning a board or roadmap.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's a translation layer from visual project dashboard to text summary.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Was there an example output conceptually?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, the chapter points to example output to show what a concise project snapshot can look like: a summary of where work sits, what's active, what's blocked or upcoming, and what deserves attention next.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When should someone use a tool like that?

speaker: ALEX
text: When the project is large, when a quick orientation is needed, when Roadmap or Board would take too long to parse nonvisually, or when you want a status check before drilling into details. It's not a replacement for direct project access, but it can be an excellent first pass.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap, let's turn the chapter's exercises into practical listening prompts.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exercise one is to create a personal tracking project. That means making a project, giving it a clear name, and adding a few items that represent your own work. The learning goal is simply getting comfortable with creation, layout switching, and item entry.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Exercise two is automation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Set up a basic automation, especially auto-add, so that matching work flows into the project without manual effort. Then verify that the rule behaves as expected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Exercise three is a sprint view.

speaker: ALEX
text: Create an iteration field, assign some items to iterations, and save a filtered view that shows the current sprint or a chosen cycle. This reinforces field creation, filtering, and saved views all together.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And exercise four goes advanced with cross-repo work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Build or modify a project so it includes multiple repositories. Then make sure the Repository field is visible and useful in your layout. Try filtering by repo and compare how one shared project can coordinate work across codebases.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we end with a few key takeaways, especially for blind and low-vision learners?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. First, Table view is usually the best primary workspace for accessibility and precision. Second, Board view is useful conceptually, but move cards by editing Status rather than dragging. Third, Roadmap is best treated as visual summary; use Table for the real data work. Fourth, custom fields are what transform Projects from a simple task list into a structured planning tool. Fifth, saved views, filters, grouping, and sorting let one project serve many different needs.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for cross-repo planning?

speaker: ALEX
text: Show the Repository field. Always. It reduces ambiguity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And one final administrative note: where should learners go for the most current behavior if GitHub's interface changes?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use GitHub Docs as the authoritative source, especially the Projects documentation, and keep an eye on the GitHub changelog. Labels and milestones docs also matter because those objects often appear inside project workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a great place to stop. GitHub Projects is much more than a board. It's a flexible planning system, and with the right habits, it can be highly workable for accessible contribution workflows too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. See you next time.
